session_metadata.title: Topics to Discuss in Future (Church Plant Planning Document)
session_metadata.session_topic: Working agenda and decision log for planting a local church: motivations, vision, governance, theological essentials, non-negotiables, finalized positions on contested doctrines (head coverings, sign gifts, eschatology, etc.), and operational to-dos.

theological_themes: vision and mission of the local church
statement of faith and theological triage
role of women in the church
Acts 2:42 marks of the church (teaching, fellowship, breaking of bread, prayer)
full-time worker support and avoiding paid pastoral model
partnership with other churches and organizations
avoiding the labels Calvinism and Arminianism
silence of women in church meetings
scope of church-order vs evangelistic meetings
role of women as titus 2 model, gospel witness, and home-keeper
Pauline correctives to early churches (gifts, doctrine, ethics, unity, eschatology)
modest dress and dress code for leadership

chunks.content: Section header: Things to discuss. The document opens as a working agenda enumerating topics that the planning team intends to work through for a future church plant. Items cover both organizational and theological matters. Color legend used in the document: yellow indicates ongoing discussion, green indicates discussion finished.
claims: The document is a working agenda for a future church plant.
Yellow-highlighted items are ongoing; green-highlighted items are finished discussions.

chunks.content: Why we want to initiate the church plant (individual reasons recorded on 25th Aug 22). Multiple individual reasons were collected. Reasons given included: need for a church in the area; locality-based church making it easier to invite people who live there; need for college ministry in the area; scope for counselling in the area; greater need for the work in the local city; a sense of God's leading; a passion or vision for biblical outworking of the church; a sense that this is the ideal context for the contributor's gifting; new believers and evangelism; leading of the Holy Spirit; desire for a church which focuses on the Word of God; opportunity among young couples; teaching of the Word, good discipleship, and sending people out; community.
claims: Multiple distinct motivations were articulated for planting the church, ranging from geographic need to spiritual gifting to the leading of the Holy Spirit.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: The plant is motivated primarily by external geographic and demographic need (need for a church in the area, locality-based access, college ministry, counselling, the broader need in the city).
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Stated as the area lacks an adequate church and there are unreached groups (students, young couples) accessible through proximity.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: The plant is motivated primarily by internal sense of divine call and gifting (sense of God's leading, vision for biblical outworking, gifting fit, leading of the Holy Spirit).
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Stated as personal conviction that this is where God is directing and where individual gifts will be exercised.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: The plant is motivated primarily by ecclesial values (focus on the Word, discipleship and sending, community, evangelism of new believers).
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Stated as a desire to embody specific marks of a healthy church not currently present.

chunks.content: Topic placeholder: Vision and Mission. Listed as a topic to be developed but no content was recorded under it in this revision of the document.
themes: vision and mission of the local church
claims: Vision and Mission is identified as a major topic still to be defined.

chunks.content: Topic placeholder: Core values and beliefs. Listed as a topic to be developed; concrete content appears later in the document under 'Core values'.
claims: Core values and beliefs is a top-level topic for the church plant.

chunks.content: Expectations and commitment ('social contract' for meetings, recorded 8th Sept 22). Members are asked to commit to involvement in all current activities as far as possible; if assigned a role, to accomplish it or find someone else who can; if absent, to take responsibility to read the notes and share concerns or thoughts; to be open to talk if anyone seems to not commit much; to give grace especially considering life stages. Participation requires taking initiative, ownership, and providing input. Meetings should also be marked by open communication and confidentiality. Meetings are held on a set time basis: if a topic needs more time, push to next meeting or, with consent of all, continue; it should be acceptable for people to leave at the scheduled end time.
claims: Members commit to active involvement, role completion, ownership, and open communication.
Absent members must read notes and share concerns afterwards.
Meetings are time-bounded; topics not finished are pushed to the next meeting unless all consent to extend.
Confidentiality and grace (accounting for life stage) are required posture.

chunks.content: Theological essentials topic. Includes: a Statement of Faith ('What we believe'); a 'triage' of what to discuss first / what the absolute minimal beliefs of the church are. Three triage items called out: the role of women in church, free will versus sovereignty, and the question of formal deacons. A resource is noted: 'Masterpiece of the Ages'.
themes: statement of faith and theological triage
role of women in the church
claims: The church needs both a full statement of faith and a triage that distinguishes essentials from secondary matters.
Three immediate triage topics are: role of women, free will vs sovereignty, formal deacons.

chunks.content: Additional topic placeholders without developed content in this section: Policies for addressing current events and issues; Roles and accountability; Legal (Registration, Name of the church); Membership; Evangelism; Church Activities; Discipleship; Songs and song book (with sub-item: Bible Study Song book).
claims: Policies, roles, legal/registration, membership, evangelism, activities, discipleship, and song book are open agenda items.

chunks.content: Sermons topic, sub-questions to discuss: Who preaches? Length of message. Content (gospel-driven versus 'Christianese'). These remain open for resolution at this point in the document; final positions appear later in the Non-Negotiables section under 'Preaching'.
claims: Sermon practice raises three sub-questions: who preaches, length, and content style.

chunks.content: Additional topic placeholders: Small group / cell group / life groups; Eldership (with note 'Need to start training course right away').
claims: Small group structure is to be defined.
An eldership training course is needed immediately.

chunks.content: Sunday format topic, sub-questions: offering process (should people go up? can people take money out?); dress code; worship (is music worship?); church layout (pulpit/seating/cross); spoken language; breaking of bread (how to do communion, should it be covered or open, flat or unleavened bread, wine, etc).
claims: Sunday format raises questions about the offering, dress, worship/music definition, layout, language, and the mechanics of communion.

chunks.content: Additional topic placeholders: Website and presence in the community; Sunday school; Budget for church / support of ministry; Meeting place on Sunday; Church discipline (e.g. not fellowship) and sin issues; Regular communication of who we are and why we do what we do; Full-time workers question; Should we have a focus on a translation?
themes: full-time worker support and avoiding paid pastoral model
claims: The church plant must decide its public presence, children's ministry, budget, meeting place, discipline policy, communications, full-time worker model, and Bible translation focus.

chunks.content: Things for training. Six training areas identified: communicating; counseling; culture; expository preaching; language; biblical eldership resources.
claims: Six training competencies are needed: communication, counseling, cultural understanding, expository preaching, language, and biblical eldership.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Meeting Format and Acts 2:42. Types of meetings include the Weekly Sunday gathering, structured around the four marks: Breaking of Bread, Teaching, Prayer, Fellowship. The format begins with reflective silence (10 minutes) and a remembrance meeting with one assigned starter.
themes: Acts 2:42 marks of the church (teaching, fellowship, breaking of bread, prayer)
claims: The weekly Sunday meeting is structured around the four Acts 2:42 marks: breaking of bread, teaching, prayer, and fellowship.
The meeting opens with a period of reflective silence and includes a remembrance meeting with an assigned starter.

chunks.content: Sample Sunday format (recorded 14th Nov 22). 9:30, 5 min reflective silence (assigned person starts). 9:35, 5 min assigned person starts the meeting. 9:40, 25 min open time (songs, exhortation, prayer). 10:05, 15 min giving thanks for bread and cup, distribution and offering. 10:20, 10 min announcements. 10:30, 5 min prayer for announced lists and for the sermon. 10:35, 15 min songs. 10:50, 40 min sermon and closing prayer by the preacher. 11:30, 15 min break. 11:45, 5 min buffer. 11:50-12:30, 40 min split track: Kids (Sunday school, done on rotational basis so they can also join the adult gathering at times); Adults (prayer time; youth meeting / family and parenting; men's and women's fellowship; singing time). Other: testimony time. Ends 12:30.
claims: The Sunday meeting runs from 9:30 to 12:30 with defined time blocks for silence, open ministry, breaking of bread, announcements, songs, sermon, break, and parallel adult/children programming.
Children's Sunday school is rotational so kids can also experience the adult gathering.

chunks.content: Other weekly activities under the meeting-format non-negotiable: Bible studies on a weekday.
claims: Beyond Sunday, the church holds weekday Bible studies.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable topic: Role of men, women, and children, flagged 'will revisit after study'. Sub-points to study: head covering; silence; teaching other women and children; practices under the church order; which meetings come under church order; singing participation for women; roles that should be encouraged for women to take up.
themes: role of women in the church
silence of women in church meetings
scope of church-order vs evangelistic meetings
claims: Roles of men, women, and children must be defined including head covering, silence, women teaching women and children, and which meetings fall under church order.

chunks.content: Point of contact (22 Jan 22). Plan: families keep in touch with youngsters. A seekers list is maintained, with each seeker assigned to a family for relational follow-up (specific names of seekers and assigned families have been redacted for anonymization). Seekers should be told officially, perhaps at the next Bible Study, that this is not a mentoring arrangement; rather, all of the families are available to them; each seeker is being assigned to a family simply for keeping in touch and being available; it can be a 'home away from home'; they should not be surprised if someone from that family calls or meets them individually. A note suggests revisiting the topic the following week. The arrangement will continue as long as the seekers are present in the area. One person was tasked with creating a document for this.
claims: Each seeker is assigned to a family for relational follow-up, explicitly not mentoring.
The arrangement is a 'home away from home' available as long as the seeker remains in the area.
Seekers will be informed of the arrangement officially, likely at a Bible study.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Membership. Reasons for having formal membership include: figuring out where prospective members are saved and how they are in their Christian life; better governance for the elders and better pastoral care; the members agreeing to the statement of faith; supporting church discipline by their willingness to be under the authority of the church; and creating a clear distinction between members, attendees, and visitors. Members enjoy specific privileges. There should be a formal celebrating public statement when someone becomes a member. The team distinguishes between three categories: legal status; church membership (which includes agreeing to the statement of faith and other commitments); and policy.
themes: statement of faith and theological triage
claims: Formal membership exists to clarify spiritual standing, enable elder oversight and pastoral care, secure agreement to the statement of faith, support church discipline, and distinguish members from attendees and visitors.
There is a public celebratory statement at the moment of joining.
Membership is distinct from legal status and from policy.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Ornaments and Marriage practices (16/2/23). On ornaments, wearing, exchanging during weddings (rings), and demanding during weddings (transactions / indirect dowry). The church should teach against harmful practices like dowry; it may be good to brief on this during weddings. On marriage practices generally: any practice that is tied to culture or religion the church should take a stand against. The Mangal sutra is raised as an open question, what is the church's stand if a Hindu convert wears one. A practice that is purely cultural and not linked to any previous religion should be carefully considered, including doing some research and asking the family why they want to do it.
claims: Wedding rings and ornament-related practices need definition.
The church teaches against harmful cultural practices like dowry and may include this in wedding briefings.
Any wedding practice tied to a non-Christian religion should be opposed.
Purely cultural (non-religious) practices should be carefully evaluated case by case after asking the family why.

chunks.content: Marriage practices (continued). Marriages done under the church should be marriages between believers. If the parties come from other backgrounds, the elders should discern. On dating: dating among unbelievers is not encouraged; dating among believers is not discouraged because many youngsters will go that route, but the church should help young people reason out why they are dating; conviction about dating is also based on the family. On marriage with a non-believer: after counselling, if they still want to marry, the church will not perform the wedding; their membership comes into question, though they may come back as a guest, and the eldership may walk alongside to see if they can be restored. Whatever discipline is given, restoration must be the key. On divorce: the church should not support divorce, must take precautionary measures to avoid it, and is to draft a position. Premarital counseling is mandatory.
claims: Church-conducted weddings are limited to marriages between believers; mixed-background cases require elder discernment.
Dating among unbelievers is discouraged; dating among believers is permitted but should be reasoned.
If a member marries a non-believer against counsel, the church will not perform the wedding and the person's membership is in question; restoration remains the goal.
Divorce is not supported; the church will define precautionary measures.
Premarital counseling is mandatory.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Finances and Transparency, Full-time worker. The church should avoid having a full-time pastor. As a church it should support those doing full-time work locally. Commendation should include financial support as needed. 1 Tim 5:17 is cited on honoring elders. The church as a whole should take responsibility for ensuring that serving the church does not end up as only one person's duty.
themes: full-time worker support and avoiding paid pastoral model
claims: The church should not have a full-time paid pastor.
The church should financially support full-time workers serving locally and include this in commendations.
1 Tim 5:17 grounds the obligation to honor elders.
Service to the church must be distributed and not fall on one person.

chunks.content: Finances and Transparency, Budget and giving. Give a gift to those who come to minister from outside, considering their financial state. The budget should align to the church vision. Have special offerings as needed. A budget should be presented for each year showing expenses and allocations. For transparency, provide a monthly update to a forum made available to members. Open questions: what is church offering used for (supporting people in need is one suggestion, needs more discussion); tithing or not; teaching about giving; whether the church gives to anyone (even outsiders) who asks or appeals; look into 80G and tax benefits.
claims: The annual budget is published with expenses and allocations and aligns to church vision.
Visiting ministers are given a gift considering their financial state.
Special offerings may be raised as needed.
Members get a monthly transparency update.
Open questions remain on use of offerings, tithing position, teaching on giving, and whether to give to any outside appeal.
The church should investigate Indian 80G tax-exemption status.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Preaching. Preaching should be given to those with the gift. Full-time workers can give a roughly ten-minute update. Teaching style is expository and systematic, with topical messages once a month based on topics determined by the elders. Elders use discretion for potential guest speakers as needed. There is an open forum for feedback to preachers. Sermons can be cut short to accommodate a 10-minute update from a full-time worker. Priority is for sermons to be given by elders, with periodic opportunities for other men from within the assembly. Books being preached through are determined by the elders thoughtfully and prayerfully depending on the need of the church. Preaching training is needed, possibly via a 'preachers club' style setup.
claims: Preaching is restricted to those with the gift and prioritized to elders, with periodic opportunities for other men.
The default style is expository and systematic, with one topical message per month chosen by the elders.
Guest speakers are at elder discretion.
Full-time workers may give roughly ten-minute updates, including by shortening the sermon.
There is a feedback forum for preachers.
Preaching books are chosen by the elders based on the church's need.
A preacher-training group should be established.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Sign Gifts. The church believes that the sign gifts have ceased, there can be a one-time occurrence, but in the sense of a continuous practice in the church they have ceased. When the topic is addressed in church, it should not point to people who believe otherwise; people who believe otherwise should use discretion when they explain it to someone from the church. The position should be mentioned in the church practices.
claims: The continuous practice of sign gifts has ceased; one-time occurrences are not denied.
Public teaching on this should not target individuals who hold continuationist views.
Continuationist members should exercise discretion when explaining their view to others in the church.
Initial position: this is to appear in the church practices.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Sign gifts have ceased as a continuous practice and the position should be stated in the church's official practices.

chunks.content: Sign Gifts, revised position after later discussion (3/8/23). The team does not have to mention this as a statement in the practices, the statement of faith, or any online document; it is only for internal understanding. Stated formulation: 'The sign gifts are currently not used in our church as an everyday practice. But God can use these gifts when he wishes for his purposes and the expansion of His kingdom.'
claims: The earlier statement on cessation will not be published in the practices, statement of faith, or online, it is for internal understanding only.
Operational position: sign gifts are not used as an everyday practice, but God may use them when He wishes for His purposes and the expansion of His kingdom.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Practical cessation: sign gifts are not the everyday practice of this church.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Reflects the team's observed practice and earlier study.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Open continuationism: God may sovereignly use sign gifts when He wishes for His purposes and the expansion of His kingdom.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Preserves divine prerogative and avoids a hard cessationist line.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Registration. Need to discuss with someone with experience who can explain the process. Decision: the team will start the registration process but also start meeting in parallel.
claims: Registration will be pursued in parallel with starting to meet, after consulting someone experienced in the process.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Partnerships with other churches or organizations. Different scenarios in view: preaching from other churches; financial support; resources support; courses or other things done in other churches that could be promoted or announced; whether to send flyers or gospel meetings to other evangelical churches. Things to consider per case: each case is decided case by case; the partner church's beliefs are considered, including how aligned and how different they are, and the same applies to speakers; why they are doing what they are doing (e.g. for a fundraiser); there must be conversation with the people and churches; if the elders are overloaded they could outsource information-gathering; the elders should take partnership seriously, engage and talk about it; the church hopes to partner with other churches and should be intentional about it.
themes: partnership with other churches and organizations
claims: All inter-church partnerships are evaluated case by case.
Doctrinal alignment of the partner church and of any speaker is the primary filter.
Motive of the partnering activity (e.g. fundraiser) must be understood.
Direct conversation with the partner is required; elders may delegate research if overloaded.
The church intends to be intentional about pursuing partnerships.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Grounds to leave the church and how to proceed biblically. Recognized grounds: irreconcilable theological and church-practice disagreements. If someone leaves over valid theological differences, they should have an opportunity to communicate their reasons to the church at the elders' discretion; either way, the elders should try to communicate so the body is aware. For personal conflicts within the body that escalate to a member not wanting to come to church, the elders should engage. For any felt need of change in church practice, the member should approach and discuss with the elders before leaving.
claims: The recognized ground for leaving is irreconcilable theological or practice disagreement.
Departing members may address the church at elder discretion; the elders are otherwise responsible for informing the body.
Personal conflicts that drive a member away require elder intervention.
Members must raise practice-change concerns with elders before leaving.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Church discipline. Anchored in Matthew 18, what does it mean to be 'a heathen or tax collector'; and 1 Corinthians 5, 'do not even eat' with such a person. For those who are repentant but whose sin has affected members in the process: take time with the case; confession to the church may be needed; there may be a discipline process; confession is also made to the persons affected by the issue. When an issue surfaces, the elders must act ASAP and not defer to their next meeting (which may be the next month), elders must be proactive. Open question: what are some reasons for discipline. The document includes an embedded image (handwritten notes on church discipline) and external reference links to bible.org's lesson on biblical church discipline (Matt 18:15-17, 1 Cor 5:1-13) and 9marks' church-discipline primer.
claims: Church discipline is grounded in Matthew 18 and 1 Corinthians 5.
Repentant offenders whose sin has affected the body still go through a process that may include confession to the church and to the persons affected.
Elders must act on discipline issues immediately, not wait for the next regular meeting.
External resources cited: bible.org and 9marks discipline primers.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Calvinism. The points of election and predestination are the points of a saved person. As a majority, none of the team holds to 5-point Calvinism. The team is addressing Election independently and is not currently addressing Calvinism. Final consensus: both the sovereignty of God in election and the free will of man are present in the team's view. A study is needed on three main points, Unconditional Election, Conditional Election, In-Christ Election. A clear view on Romans 9-11 is needed. The terms 'Calvinism' and 'Arminianism' will not be used in the team's documents. The team will not set a perspective 'as a church' until the three points of election are considered.
themes: avoiding the labels Calvinism and Arminianism
claims: Election and predestination apply to those who are saved.
The team rejects 5-point Calvinism.
Election is addressed on its own terms, not under the Calvinism/Arminianism labels.
Both divine sovereignty and human free will are affirmed.
A study on unconditional, conditional, and in-Christ election is required.
A clear position on Romans 9-11 is required.
The terms 'Calvinism' and 'Arminianism' will not appear in church documents.
No church-level position will be set until the three points of election are studied.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Unconditional Election, God's choice of the elect is not based on any condition foreseen in them.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Listed as one of three election views requiring study before a church-level position.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Conditional Election, God's choice of the elect is conditioned on something (commonly foreseen faith).
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Listed as one of three election views requiring study before a church-level position.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: In-Christ Election, Election is corporate and 'in Christ' rather than individual selection apart from Him.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Listed as one of three election views requiring study before a church-level position.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable topic: Head coverings and women speaking; church-order vs non-church-order meetings, first contributor's notes. According to 1 Timothy 2:8-15, by design certain roles exist between men and women; men are encouraged to take responsibility and leadership based on the example of created order. 1 Corinthians 14:34-35 expresses similar roles within a local church. The contributor holds the view typically known as 'complementarianism', wherein men and women both have leadership roles in different contexts. First takeaway: women are not to teach or have authority over men, so any praying, prophesying, or exercising of spiritual gifts in ministry toward others would have to be done outside the recognized gathering of the church. Head coverings appear to be a symbol of these roles being acknowledged. 1 Corinthians 11 gives further clarity on why a head covering is worn and why men should not cover their heads, but the contributor is uncertain how it practically plays out, it is meant to display authority in the context of prayer and prophecy in the presence of other men, yet women are to remain silent in the recognized church meeting; therefore the contributor is unsure whether head coverings are needed in the church context. It does seem clear that they are to be worn in any context where women pray or prophesy when men are present. Resolution: to make the most sense of the two scenarios, either 'silence' in church refers to judging prophecy or to the act of teaching and not to praying or prophesying, meaning women should be allowed to pray and prophesy in the recognized church context as long as their head is covered; otherwise, if they are simply remaining silent, the contributor does not see how a head covering is justified or expected based on these passages. References cited: complementarianism explainer at thegospelcoalition.org and women-pastors article at gotquestions.org.
themes: role of women in the church
silence of women in church meetings
scope of church-order vs evangelistic meetings
claims: Distinct roles between men and women exist by design (1 Tim 2:8-15) and apply within the local church (1 Cor 14:34-35).
The contributor's position is complementarianism: men and women both lead, in different contexts.
Women are not to teach or have authority over men; therefore praying, prophesying, and exercising gifts toward others happens outside the recognized gathering of the church.
Head coverings symbolize acknowledgement of these roles (1 Cor 11).
The practical application of the head covering is unclear because 1 Cor 11 ties it to prayer/prophecy with men present, while 1 Cor 14 prescribes silence in the gathered meeting.

Possible reconciliation: 'silence' refers to judging prophecy or teaching, allowing women to pray or prophesy in the gathered meeting if covered; otherwise the head covering's necessity in the meeting is not clearly justified.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Silence in 1 Cor 14 refers to judging prophecy or teaching; women may pray and prophesy in the gathered church meeting if their head is covered.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Best resolves the tension between 1 Cor 11 (covered while praying/prophesying with men present) and 1 Cor 14 (silence in church).

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Silence in 1 Cor 14 means women are simply silent in the gathered meeting, in which case the head covering's necessity in the meeting is not clearly grounded in these passages.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: If women are silent, there is no praying or prophesying for the covering to symbolize, leaving the in-meeting requirement unjustified by the texts cited.

chunks.content: Head coverings, second contributor's thoughts. Five reasons offered for the head covering: (1) Nascent trinitarianism / head hierarchy at four levels, God, Christ, Man, Woman. (2) Creation, the order of creation. (3) General revelation, natural; women to have long hair. (4) Angelology, for the sake of the angels, who are also looking at and present at worship. (5) Church practice, practiced by all churches in the area. There are valid reasons for women to keep their heads covered during the gathering of the church, especially for worship; it is a symbol of submission. The contributor thinks this applies for all worship meetings since it represents the reasons listed. Acknowledged tension: Paul talks about praying and prophesying when that is something women shouldn't be doing anyway, so why mention it, the contributor is studying this further but has not landed on an accurate answer. The contributor also wants the church to define the positive roles of women, not just what is not their role. (A linked Google Doc with the contributor's full thoughts is referenced; URL preserved as a resource link.)
themes: role of women in the church
claims: Head covering is supported by five reasons: nascent trinitarianism (four-level head hierarchy), creation order, general revelation (long hair), angelology, and universal church practice in the area.
Women should cover their heads during worship gatherings as a symbol of submission.
Open puzzle: Paul addresses praying and prophesying women, but if they should not be praying/prophesying in the meeting, the discussion of covering during these acts requires further study.
The church should articulate positive roles for women, not only restrictions.

chunks.content: Head coverings, third contributor's thoughts. Section A. Man-woman order. 1 Cor 11:8-9 and 1 Cor 11:3 establish that the head of every man is Christ, the head of the woman is the man, and the head of Christ is God. The glory of Christ is symbolically amplified by the double covering of the women. In this setting Paul (verses 8, 9) draws an unavoidable comparison between Christ and His bride: just as it would be uncharacteristic for the bride to teach and lead Christ, it is uncharacteristic for women to teach and lead in the setting of the church. This order is presumed in 1 Cor 14:34-35, women are to keep silence in the churches and ask their husbands at home if they want to learn anything. Verse 35 encourages them not to be silent but to ask, when home order is applicable.
themes: silence of women in church meetings
scope of church-order vs evangelistic meetings
claims: 1 Cor 11:3 establishes the headship order: God over Christ, Christ over man, man over woman.
1 Cor 11:8-9 grounds the order in creation: the woman is from and for the man.
The double covering of women symbolically amplifies the glory of Christ.
Paul's analogy is between Christ and His bride: as the bride does not teach or lead Christ, women do not teach or lead in the setting of the church.
1 Cor 14:34-35 presumes this order, silence in church but free questioning at home.

chunks.content: Role of women, third contributor's positive list (B). (1) As a role model to younger women, Titus 2:4-5: teach the young women to love their husbands and children, to be discreet, chaste, keepers at home, good, obedient. (2) As doer of good works, 1 Tim 2:9-10: women adorn themselves in modest apparel with shamefacedness and sobriety, with good works. (3) As saved through childbearing, 1 Tim 2:15. (4) As keeper of the home, Titus 2:5. (5) As dispenser of private instruction, Aquila and Priscilla, Acts 18:2,18,26; Rom 16:3; 1 Cor 16:19; 2 Tim 4:19. (6) As servants of the Lord. (7) As gospel witnesses, Phil 4:3. (8) As examples of holy living. (9) As sufferers for Christ's sake. (10) As worshippers.
themes: role of women in the church
role of women as titus 2 model, gospel witness, and home-keeper
claims: Women's biblical roles include: role model to younger women (Titus 2:4-5), doer of good works (1 Tim 2:9-10), saved through childbearing (1 Tim 2:15), keeper of the home (Titus 2:5), private instruction in the manner of Priscilla (Acts 18; Rom 16:3; 1 Cor 16:19; 2 Tim 4:19), servant of the Lord, gospel witness (Phil 4:3), example of holy living, sufferer for Christ, and worshipper.

chunks.content: Final consensus on Head covering, Role of a woman, and Church order / home order (28/7/23). HEAD COVERING, women should cover their head in all church-initiated meetings when men are present; for meetings where men are not present, they don't have to. Other meetings conducted for evangelistic purposes, especially outside the church, do not require head covering. ROLE OF WOMEN, women are to remain silent and not be in any leadership position or any teaching/preaching role, especially during the whole gathering of the church. In corporate worship during breaking of bread, or other meetings of the church when men are present, women should not do any leading/teaching role that represents the whole church. Exceptions are meetings like Sunday school or women's meetings where the actual overall responsibility lies with the elders or another man assigned by the elders to lead that ministry. CHURCH ORDER vs HOME ORDER, all meetings of the church will follow the church order that the elders have put in place out of the Scriptures; the exception may be evangelistic meetings. An external example is cited from another fellowship's statement: an assembly cannot function properly without the ministry of women; God has given speaking and serving gifts to women as well as men, but has placed restrictions on the leadership of women in meetings of the local church, this is not arbitrary or a comment on spirituality or giftedness but a scriptural mandate relating to church order; consequently women (qualified by spiritual gift) are able to lead women's Bible study classes, disciple other women, and teach children, and may be involved in evangelism, hospitality, and other ministries that do not require them to exercise authority over men. A manifestation of women's submission to God's designed order is the wearing of a head covering in meetings of the church where both men and women are present (1 Corinthians 11:1-16).
themes: role of women in the church
silence of women in church meetings
scope of church-order vs evangelistic meetings
claims: Final position: head coverings are required in all church-initiated meetings where men are present; not required where men are absent or in external evangelistic meetings.
Final position: women are silent and do not hold leadership/teaching/preaching roles in the gathered church.
Exception: women may lead in Sunday school or women's meetings where overall responsibility remains with the elders or a man assigned by the elders.
All church meetings follow the elders' church order from Scripture; evangelistic meetings may be the exception.
An external assembly's statement is cited as an aligned example: women's submission is manifested by head covering in mixed meetings (1 Cor 11:1-16).

chunks.content: Goals of the church, selected items. Dressing modestly, also for those who are leading. Baptism: will be done as soon as possible; will require a signed affidavit; will encourage believers to be publicly baptized (they should be willing not to be secret believers); decided case by case; should have a class and discussion with the elders before baptism.
themes: modest dress and dress code for leadership
claims: Modest dress is expected of all and especially of those leading.
Baptism is administered as soon as possible after evaluation, with a signed affidavit, a baptism class, and elder discussion.
Public baptism is encouraged; secret discipleship is discouraged.
Baptism decisions are case by case.

chunks.content: Things to do before we start. Find a meeting place, fallback options are starting at one of two homes; an alternative venue option is also under consideration. Plan a two-month schedule. Finish discussions. Conduct baptism. Plan inauguration program. Decide on a name. Produce a song book. Acquire mics and projector. Official launch dates: September 16-17, 2023, 16th: inauguration meeting and baptism (at one of two named venues); 17th: first Sunday meeting.
claims: Pre-launch tasks include venue, two-month schedule, finishing discussions, baptism, inauguration plan, name, song book, and audio-visual equipment.
Official launch is set for 16-17 September 2023, with inauguration and baptism on the 16th and the first Sunday meeting on the 17th.

chunks.content: Non-negotiable: Eschatology (Covenant / Dispensations). Open question: are the promises for Israel only, and if so, will the New Jerusalem be on earth or in heaven? One contributor's main concern: the church has not replaced Israel, all in agreement on this point. CHURCH POSITION: As a church, we will teach a pre-tribulation rapture followed by 7 years of tribulation, after which there will be a literal 1,000-year reign on earth. Stance pattern: teach one side and acknowledge other views, but have a clear stance as a church. Embedded restatement of the cessation non-negotiable also appears in this section: the church believes that the sign gifts have ceased.
claims: The church has not replaced Israel, agreed by all.
Open: are the OT promises for Israel only? Will the New Jerusalem be on earth or in heaven?
Church position: pre-tribulation rapture, followed by 7 years of tribulation, followed by a literal 1,000-year earthly reign.
Pedagogical stance: teach one side, acknowledge other views, hold a clear church stance.

chunks.content: Sign gifts, non-negotiable position recorded 27-July-23 (further explanation). The topic of sign gifts (a.k.a. miraculous or charismatic gifts), whether they have ceased, is a theological debate among Christians. 'Sign gifts' refers to certain spiritual gifts in the New Testament, particularly in 1 Corinthians 12:7-11 and 1 Corinthians 14:1-40, including prophecy, speaking in tongues, and the interpretation of tongues. CESSATIONISM: the belief that certain spiritual gifts, including sign gifts, ceased to be active after the apostolic age or the completion of the biblical canon. Their purpose was primarily to authenticate the authority of the apostles and the early church and to establish the foundation of the Christian faith; once the church was established and the New Testament was complete, these gifts were no longer necessary and gradually ceased. CONTINUATIONISM: the belief that all spiritual gifts, including sign gifts, continue to be active in the church throughout history; there is no biblical evidence or theological reason to suggest they have ceased; the Holy Spirit still empowers believers with these gifts for the edification and advancement of the church today. Note: sincere and faithful Christians hold both views; the debate is intramural; while significant, the topic is not a primary doctrine of the Christian faith and believers can find common ground in the essentials.
claims: Cessationism holds the sign gifts ceased after the apostolic age / completion of the canon because their purpose was foundational and authenticating.
Continuationism holds the sign gifts continue throughout history with no biblical reason to think otherwise.
The cessation/continuation debate is intramural and not a primary Christian doctrine.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Cessationism: sign gifts ceased after the apostolic age / canon completion; their purpose was to authenticate the apostles and establish the church's foundation.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Once the church was established and the New Testament complete, the gifts were no longer necessary and gradually ceased.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Continuationism: all spiritual gifts including sign gifts continue throughout history; the Spirit still empowers believers with them for edification and advancement of the church today.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: No biblical evidence or theological reason to claim the gifts ceased.

chunks.content: Continuation on the ceasing of spiritual gifts as per 1 Corinthians 13:8-10 (1st Aug 23). Commentary: Paul addresses different churches; concerning the church at Corinth, we see misuse of spiritual gifts and Paul's confrontation on the misuse and his focus on love (1 Cor 13:8-10). 'When the perfect comes gifts will cease' is read as occurring after the return of the Perfector. Focus on 1 Corinthians 14:1, where Paul tells believers to eagerly desire spiritual gifts. 1 Corinthians 14:39-40, be eager to prophesy, do not forbid speaking in tongues, and worship is to be orderly. Argument: if we adopt a view of cessation or practical cessation, we will not desire the spiritual gifts that Paul tells us to be eager about.
claims: 1 Cor 13:8-10's 'when the perfect comes' refers to the return of the Perfector (Christ), not the closing of the canon.
Paul commands believers to eagerly desire spiritual gifts (1 Cor 14:1).
Paul forbids forbidding tongues and commands orderly worship (1 Cor 14:39-40).
A cessationist or practically cessationist position contradicts Paul's command to eagerly desire the gifts.

chunks.content: Survey: problems Paul addressed in his letters to the churches. (1) MISUSE OF SPIRITUAL GIFTS, in Corinth and others, Paul addressed the misuse of spiritual gifts, especially tongues, providing guidance for edification rather than confusion (1 Cor 12-14). (2) DOCTRINAL ERRORS, Paul confronted false teachings, heresies, and distortions of the gospel; e.g. Galatians on adding legalistic practices to salvation by grace through faith; Colossians on syncretistic beliefs mixing other religions with Christianity. (3) MORAL AND ETHICAL PROBLEMS, sexual immorality (1 Cor 5), divisions and quarrels (1 Cor 1-3), dishonesty (Eph 4:25-32), lack of love and compassion (Phil 4:2-3). (4) DISUNITY AND DIVISION, encouraged unity and peaceful resolution; in 1 Corinthians, divisions over leader-preferences (Paul, Apollos, Cephas). (5) PERSECUTION AND OPPOSITION, letters strengthening churches under Jewish authorities and broader societal opposition. (6) TEACHING ON CHRISTIAN LIVING, love, humility, forgiveness, generosity, prayer, daily faithful following of Christ. (7) CONCERNS ABOUT THE END TIMES, in Thessalonians, the return of Christ and the resurrection of the dead.
themes: Pauline correctives to early churches (gifts, doctrine, ethics, unity, eschatology)
claims: Paul's letters address seven recurring problem categories: misuse of gifts, doctrinal error, moral/ethical issues, disunity, persecution, Christian living, and end-time concerns.
The Corinthian misuse of gifts is paradigmatic of category (1).
Galatians and Colossians are paradigmatic of category (2): legalism added to grace and syncretism with other religions.
Thessalonians is the paradigmatic letter for end-time concerns.

chunks.content: Dating among believers, non-negotiable position (27-July-23). Dating among believers will not be discouraged because many youngsters will take that route, though it is not encouraged either. Standalone topic flagged but not yet developed: stand on alcohol consumption.
claims: Dating among believers is permitted but not encouraged.
The church's stand on alcohol consumption is an open agenda item.

chunks.content: Things to finalize, status snapshot. Head coverings, DONE. Role of women, silence (of leading) in the presence of men, DONE. Dress code, business casual for leadership given the heart of demonstrating godliness, DONE. Dates, Jan 14th. Schedule item: potential breakfast meeting with a prospective member on Monday the 6th. Vision goals (open). Core values: (a) Bible, expository preaching and teaching; (b) Christ-centred, Lord's Supper; (c) Fellowship, holistic small groups, loving relationships; (d) Evangelism; (e) Discipleship; (f) Worship; (g) Developing and empowering leadership; (h) Gift-oriented ministry; (i) Prayer; (j) Mission focused; (k) Good works (social work). Logistics: RSVP for Inauguration program and have a letter signed by the elders. Place, name, and registration: church name proposed (a regional Bible Fellowship name). Trustee options under consideration (specific personal names redacted).
themes: modest dress and dress code for leadership
role of women in the church
vision and mission of the local church
claims: Head coverings, role-of-women silence in mixed meetings, and dress code (business casual for leadership) are finalized.

Eleven core values are adopted: Bible (expository), Christ-centred (Lord's Supper), Fellowship (holistic small groups), Evangelism, Discipleship, Worship, Developing/empowering leadership, Gift-oriented ministry, Prayer, Mission-focused, Good works.
An inauguration RSVP letter is to be signed by the elders.
A church name has been proposed and trustee candidates are being weighed.

chunks.content: Open topic flags and song-book operations. Open topics for further study: Holy Spirit; Worship. Song book / app / music plan: facilitate thematic selection; create a PDF; eventually print hard copies; start with 50 copies and ask for old books; investigate permission to print another fellowship's PDF; work on producing the church's own hard copy after 6 months; print 10 copies of another fellowship's song book; obtain a copy of 'Songs of Zion'. Sunday school: open. Logistical norm: airplane mode and silence phones in the meeting.
claims: Topics on the Holy Spirit and Worship remain open for study.
Song-book strategy: assemble a thematic PDF, start with 50 hard copies (sourced from existing books or another fellowship's PDF), then produce the church's own after six months.
Phones are silenced and put on airplane mode during the meeting.

chunks.content: To-do list for launch. Prepare the inauguration letter. Send text to elders of other assemblies (one elder of another assembly was already informed); send to other churches; wait 3-4 days and then send to others. Plan the inaugural program. Plan sermon/teaching for the first two months. See if a website can be obtained before launch. Hold a meeting of all who want to join, to explain how the church will function, covering head covering, statement of faith on marriage, and dress code (business casual for leadership given the heart of demonstrating godliness). For one prospective baptismal candidate (name redacted): topics to cover before baptism include the statement of faith, church practices, disciplines, marriage, baptism; a side conversation about a relational situation is also planned; the conversation will happen after the team returns. Preaching and teaching for the next three months: 2nd meeting on the statement of faith, with possibly the last week being singing and testimony time; first sermon, continuation in John; Bible study and sermon to continue from where the prior series left off in John, taken by the same person.
themes: statement of faith and theological triage
claims: Inaugural-letter outreach proceeds in waves: peer-elder texts first, then other churches after 3-4 days.
A pre-launch town-hall will explain head covering, marriage statement, and dress code to prospective members.
The first preaching series at launch is a continuation in the Gospel of John, taught by the same person who taught the prior series.
Pre-baptism preparation for a candidate covers the statement of faith, church practices, discipline, marriage, and baptism.

embedded_assets.description: Handwritten notes on church discipline embedded under the Church Discipline non-negotiable section.

raw_extracted_text:
Things to discuss

1. Why we want to initiate the church plant (Individuals)
  a. 25th Aug 22
    i. Need for a church in this area / Locality based church / Easier to invite people who are in this area.
    ii. Need for college ministry in the area
    iii. Scope for counselling in the area
    iv. Greater need for the work in the local city
    v. A sense of God's leading
    vi. A passion / vision for biblical outworking of the church
    vii. A sense that it is the ideal context for my gifting
    viii. New believers / Evangelism
    ix. Leading of the Holy Spirit
    x. A church which focuses on the Word of God
    xi. Opportunity among young couples
    xii. Teaching of the word, good discipleship and send them out
    xiii. Community

2. Vision/Mission
3. Core values and beliefs
4. Expectations/Commitment
  - 'Social contract', expectations during meetings and how to engage with each other
  - 8th Sept 22
    - Involvement: committing to be involved in all current activities as much as possible; if assigned a role, accomplish it or find someone who can; if absent for a meeting, take responsibility to read notes and share concerns; if anyone seems to not commit much, be open to talk about it; give grace especially considering life stages.
    - Participation: taking initiative; ownership; provide input.
    - Open communication.
    - Confidentiality.
    - Meetings decided on a set time basis (if a topic needs more time, push to next meeting or with consent of all continue; should be OK if people leave after the time).

5. Theological essentials
  a. Statement of faith, what we believe.
  b. Triage, what to discuss first / absolute minimal beliefs of the church: role of women in church; free will / sovereignty; formal deacons.
  c. Resource, Masterpiece of the ages.

6. Policies for addressing current events and issues.
7. Roles and accountability.
8. Legal: a. Registration. b. Name of the church.
9. Membership.
10. Evangelism.
11. Church Activities.
12. Discipleship.
13. Songs and song book, Bible Study Song book.
14. Sermons: a. Who preaches? b. Length of message etc. c. Content (gospel / Christianese).
15. Small group / cell group / life groups.
16. Eldership, Need to start training course right away.
17. Sunday format: a. Offering process (should people go up? can people take money out?). b. Dress code? c. Worship? (Is music worship?). d. Church layout (pulpit / seating / cross). e. Spoken Language. f. Breaking of Bread, how to do communion? should it be covered or open? flat, unleavened bread, wine, etc?
18. Website and presence in the community.
19. Sunday school.
20. Budget for church, support of ministry.
21. Meeting place on Sunday.
22. Church discipline (e.g. not fellowship) and sin issues.
23. Regular communication of who we are and why we do what we do.
24. Full time workers?
25. Should we have a focus on a translation?

Things for training
1. Communicating
2. Counseling
3. Culture
4. Expository preaching
5. Language
6. Biblical eldership resources

(Yellow, ongoing) (Green, finished discussing)

Non-negotiables:
1. Meeting Format & 2:42
  a. Types of meetings, Weekly Sunday: Breaking of Bread, Teaching, Prayer, Fellowship.
  b. Format, Reflective silence (10 min); Remembrance meeting with one assigned starter.
  c. Sample format (14th Nov 22): 9:30 5min reflective silence (assigned person starts); 9:35 5min assigned person starts the meeting; 9:40 25min open time (songs, exhortation, prayer); 10:05 15min giving thanks for bread & cup, distribution and offering; 10:20 10min announcements; 10:30 5min prayer for announced lists and for sermon; 10:35 15min songs; 10:50 40min sermon and closing prayer by preacher; 11:30 15min break; 11:45 5min buffer; 11:50-12:30 40min Kids (Sunday school, rotational so they can also join the gathering for adults); Adults (Prayer time; Youth meeting / Family & Parenting; Men's & Women's Fellowship; Singing time); Other: testimony time. Ends 12:30.
  Other weekly activities: Bible studies on weekday.

2. Role of men, women, and children (will revisit after study): head covering; silence; teaching other women and children; practices under church order; which meetings come under church order; singing participation for women; roles to encourage for women.

3. Point of contact (22 Jan 22): families keeping in touch with youngsters. Seekers list with assigned families (specific names redacted). Plan to tell seekers officially, possibly at next Bible Study: not mentoring; all of us are available to you; we are assigning each of you to a family for keeping in touch and being available; can be home away from home; don't be surprised if someone calls or meets individually. Next week will talk about it. For how long, till they are here. One person will create the doc.

4. Membership. Why: figure out where they are saved and how they are in their Christian life; better governance for elders, better pastoral care; members agree to the statement of faith; for church discipline, willing to be under the authority of the church; for distinction between members, attendees, and visitors. Privileges of members. Have a formal celebrating public statement when someone becomes a member. Differentiate: Legal; Church membership (agreeing also to the statement of faith and other things); Policy.

5. Ornaments & Marriage practices (16/2/23). Ornaments: wearing; exchanging during wedding (rings); demanding during wedding, transactions / indirect dowry, church should teach against harmful practices like dowry, may be good to brief during weddings. For marriages: any practice tied to culture/religion we should take a stand against; Mangal sutra, what is our stand if a Hindu comes with one; a cultural practice not linked to any previous religion should be carefully considered, doing research and asking the family why they want to do it. Marriage practices: marriage done under the church should be among believers; if from other backgrounds, elders should discern. Dating: among unbelievers, not encouraged; among believers, wouldn't discourage because many youngsters will take that route, help youngsters to reason out why dating, conviction also based on the family. Marriage with a non-believer: after counselling if they still want to marry, the church will not do the wedding; their membership will be in question, they may come back as a guest, but the eldership can walk alongside and see if they can be restored. Whatever discipline is given, restoration should be key. Divorce: should not support divorce; precautionary measures to avoid; should make a [position]. Premarital counseling should be mandatory.

6. Finances & Transparency. Full time worker (not necessarily supporting church full time): should avoid having a full time pastor; as a church should support those doing full time work locally; commendation should include financial support as needed; 1 Tim 5:17 honor elders; church as a whole should take responsibility for ensuring serving the church doesn't end up as only one person's duty. Budget: give gift to those who come to minister from outside considering their financial state; should have a budget that aligns to church vision; have special offerings as needed; should have a budget presented for each year showing expenses and allocations. Transparency: monthly update to a forum made available to members. What we use church offering for: supporting people who are in need?, needs more talk. Tithing or not. Teaching about giving. Do we give to anyone (even outsiders) who asks or appeals? Look into 80G and tax benefits.

7. Preaching. Should be given to those with the gift. Full time workers can maybe give ten minute update. Expository teaching systematically with topical once a month based on topics determined by the elders. Elders' discretion for potential guest speakers as needed. Open forum for feedback to preachers. Cut sermon short to accommodate full time workers giving 10 minute update. Priority for sermons given by elders, periodic opportunities for other men from within the assembly. Books determined by elders thoughtfully and prayerfully depending on need of the church. Should have training for preaching and a 'preachers club' setup.

8. Sign Gifts. The church believes the sign gifts have ceased. There can be one time occurrence but in the sense of continuous practice it has ceased. When talking about it in church: not to point to people who believe otherwise; people who believe otherwise to use discretion when they explain to someone from church. So it has to be mentioned in the church practices.
After discussion (3/8/23): we don't have to mention it as a statement in the practices, statement of faith, or any online document, only for our understanding. The sign gifts are currently not used in our church as an everyday practice. But God can use these gifts when he wishes for his purposes and the expansion of His kingdom.

9. Registration. Need to discuss with someone with experience who can explain. We will start the process but start meeting.

10. Partnerships with other churches or organization. Different scenarios: preaching from other churches; financial support; resources support; courses or other things done in other churches will be promoted or announced; will we send flyers or gospel meetings to other evangelical churches. Things to consider: each case depends case to case and should be discussed; church-to-church beliefs should be considered, if aligned similarly and how different they are, same applies to the speakers; why they are doing what they are doing, like for a fundraiser; there has to be a conversation with people and churches; if the elders are overloaded, they could outsource the work and get more information; the elders should take it seriously, engage and talk about it; we are hoping to partner with other churches and should be intentional about it.

11. Grounds to leave and how would we proceed Biblically? Irreconcilable theological and church practice disagreements. If someone leaves with valid theological differences, they should have an opportunity to communicate their reasons to the church at the elders' discretion. Either way the elders should try to communicate so the body is aware. For personal conflicts within the body, if it gets to a point where a member does not want to come to church, the elders should get engaged. For any felt need of change in church practice, the member should approach and discuss with the elders before leaving.

12. Church discipline. Matt 18, what does it mean to be a heathen or tax collector. 1 Cor 5, 'do not even eat' with such. For those who are repentant but it has affected members in the process: there should be time taken with the case; confession to the church may be needed; may be discipline process; confession to those persons who have been affected by the issue. When an issue comes out the elders have to act ASAP and not keep it for their next meeting (which may be even the next month). The elders have to be proactive. What are some reasons for discipline? [embedded image: handwritten notes]. References: bible.org Lesson 16 (Biblical Church Discipline, Matt 18:15-17, 1 Cor 5:1-13); 9marks church-discipline primer.

13. Calvinism. The points of election and predestination are the points of a saved person. As a majority, none of us hold to 5 point Calvinism. We are addressing Election independently and not currently addressing Calvinism. Final consensus: both the sovereignty of God in election and the free will of man are present in our view. Need to have a study on the 3 main points: Unconditional Election, Conditional Election, In-Christ Election. Need a clear view on Romans 9-11. We will not use the terms 'calvinism' or 'arminianism' in our documents. We will not set a perspective 'as a church' until the 3 points are considered.

14. Head coverings and women speaking AND church order meetings and non-church order meetings.

First contributor's notes: According to 1 Timothy 2:8-15, by design certain roles exist between men and women. Men are encouraged to boldly take responsibility and leadership based on the example of created order. In 1 Corinthians 14:34-35 there is a similar expression of these roles within a local church. Hold the view typically known as complementarianism, men and women both have leadership roles in different contexts. Women are not to teach or have authority over men; therefore any praying, prophesying, and exercising of spiritual gifts in ministry toward others would have to be done outside of the recognized gathering of the church. Head coverings appear to be a symbol of these roles being acknowledged. 1 Corinthians 11 presents further clarity as to why a head covering is worn and why men should not cover their heads, but it is not certain how it practically plays out since it is meant to display authority in the context of prayer and prophesy in the presence of other men but yet women are to remain silent in the recognized church meeting; therefore not sure whether head coverings are needed in the church context. It does seem clear that they are to be worn in any context where women pray or prophesy when men are present. To make most sense of these two scenarios, either 'silence' in church refers to judging prophecy or to the act of teaching and not to praying or prophesying, meaning women should be allowed to do so in the recognized church context as long as their head is covered. If that is not the case, and if they are remaining silent, then the contributor doesn't see how a head covering is justified/expected based on these passages. References: thegospelcoalition.org complementarianism-for-dummies; gotquestions.org women-pastors.

Second contributor's thoughts: The reasons for head covering are: 1. Nascent trinitarianism, head hierarchy, 4 levels, God, Christ, Man, Woman. 2. Creation, order of creation. 3. General Revelation, natural, woman to have long hair. 4. Angelology, for sake of angels (angels are also looking and present at worship). 5. Church practice, practiced by all churches in the area. There are some valid reasons for woman to keep their head covered during the gathering of the church especially for worship. It is a symbol in showing submission. This is for all times during worship meetings since it represents the reasons mentioned above. But Paul talks about praying and prophesying when that is something they shouldn't be doing anyways then why talk about it. Studying more on this but haven't been able to get an accurate answer. Another thing we have to define is the roles of women in the church, we are so focused on what is not their roles and would be interested in focusing on this. (Linked Google Doc with full thoughts.)

Third contributor's thoughts:
A. Man-woman order. [1Co 11:8-9] For the man is not of the woman; but the woman of the man. Neither was the man created for the woman; but the woman for the man. [1Co 11:3] But I would have you know, that the head of every man is Christ; and the head of the woman is the man; and the head of Christ is God. The glory of Christ is symbolically amplified by the double covering of the women. In this setting Paul says verses 8,9 drawing unavoidable comparison between Christ and His bride. Just as it would be uncharacteristic for the bride to teach and lead Christ, it is uncharacteristic for women to teach and lead in the setting of the church. This order is presumed in [1Co 14:34-35] Let your women keep silence in the churches; for it is not permitted unto them to speak; but they are commanded to be under obedience, as also saith the law. And if they will learn any thing, let them ask their husbands at home: for it is a shame for women to speak in the church. v.35 encourages them to not be silent but ask when home order is applicable.

B. Role of women: 1. As a role model to younger women, Tit 2:4-5. 2. As doer of good works, 1 Ti 2:9-10. 3. As saved through childbearing, 1 Ti 2:15. 4. As keeper of the home, Titus 2:5. 5. As dispenser of private instruction (Aquila and Priscilla, Acts 18:2,18,26; Rom 16:3; 1 Cor 16:19; 2 Tim 4:19). 6. As servants of the Lord. 7. As gospel witnesses, Phil 4:3. 8. As examples of holy living. 9. As sufferers for Christ's sake. 10. As worshippers.

Final thoughts on Head covering, Role of a woman and Church order/home order (28/7/23):
- Head Covering: Women should cover their head in all church-initiated meetings when men are present. For meetings when men are not present, they don't have to. Other meetings conducted for evangelistic purposes especially outside the church, women don't have to cover their head.
- Role of women: Women are to remain silent and not be in any leadership position or teaching/preaching role especially during the whole gathering of the church. In corporate worship during breaking of bread or other meetings of the church especially when men are present, woman should not do any leading/teaching role which represents the whole church. Exceptions: meetings like Sunday school or women's meeting where the actual overall responsibility is with the elders or another man assigned by the elders to lead that ministry.
- Church order/home order: All meetings of the church will follow church order that the elders have put in place based out of the scriptures. The exception maybe for evangelistic meetings.

Example of an aligned external assembly's statement: An assembly cannot function properly without the ministry of women. God has given to women as well as to men both speaking and serving gifts; however, God has placed restrictions on the leadership of women in meetings of the local church. This is not an arbitrary choice, nor does it reflect less spirituality or giftedness; it is a scriptural mandate relating to church order. Consequently, women (qualified by their particular spiritual gift) are able to lead women's Bible study classes, disciple other women, and teach children. They may also be involved in evangelism, hospitality and other ministries which do not require them to exercise authority over men. A manifestation of women's submission to God's designed order is the wearing of a head covering in meetings of the church where both men and women are present (1 Corinthians 11:1-16).

Goals of the church: Dressing modestly and also for those who are leading. Baptism: will be done as soon as possible; will want signed affidavit; will encourage believers to be publicly baptized (should be willing to not be secret believers); case by case; should have a class and have discussion with elders.

Things to do before we start: Figure place, if not start at one of two homes; alternative venue option. Two months schedule. Finish discussion. Baptism. Inauguration program. Name. Song book. Mics, projector. Official Sept 16-17, 2023, 16th: inauguration meeting and baptism (one of two named venues); 17th: 1st Sunday meeting.

15. Eschatology (Covenant/Dispensations). Promises are for Israel only? Will New Jerusalem be on earth or in heaven? One contributor's main concern: the church has not replaced Israel, all in agreement. As a church we will teach a pre-trib rapture followed by 7 years of tribulation after which there will be a literal 1000 year reign on earth. Teach one side and acknowledge other views but have a clear stance as a church.

This church believes that the sign gifts have ceased.

Non-Negotiable (27-July-23), Sign gifts. The topic of sign gifts (miraculous or charismatic gifts) and whether they have ceased is a theological debate. 'Sign gifts' refers to certain spiritual gifts in 1 Corinthians 12:7-11 and 1 Corinthians 14:1-40, prophecy, tongues, interpretation of tongues, etc.
Cessationism: certain spiritual gifts including sign gifts ceased to be active after the apostolic age or the completion of the biblical canon. The purpose of these miraculous gifts was primarily to authenticate the authority of the apostles and the early church and to establish the foundation of the Christian faith. Once the church was established and the New Testament was complete, these sign gifts were no longer necessary and gradually ceased.
Continuationism: all spiritual gifts including sign gifts continue to be active in the church throughout history. There is no biblical evidence or theological reason to suggest these gifts have ceased. The Holy Spirit still empowers believers with these gifts for the edification and advancement of the church today.
Note: sincere and faithful Christians hold both views; the debate is intramural; while significant, it is not a primary doctrine of the Christian faith and believers can find common ground in the essentials of Christianity.

In continuous to ceasing of spiritual gifts as per 1 Corinthians 13:8-10 (1st Aug 23). Commentary around Paul addressing different churches. Concerning the Church of Corinthians, we see misuse of spiritual gifts and Paul's confrontation on the misuse and focus on love. 1 Corinthians 13:8-10. When the perfect comes gifts will cease which is after the return of the Perfector. Focus on 1st Corinthians 14:1, where Paul talks about eagerly desiring spiritual gift. 1st Corinthians 14:39-40, be eager to prophesy but do not forbid speaking in tongues and also talks about orderly worship. If we take a view of cessation or practical cessation, we will not desire for the spiritual gifts that Paul refers to be eager about.

Paul's letters address common problems: 1. Misuse of spiritual gifts (1 Cor 12-14). 2. Doctrinal errors, Galatians (legalism added to grace), Colossians (syncretism). 3. Moral and ethical problems, sexual immorality (1 Cor 5), divisions and quarrels (1 Cor 1-3), dishonesty (Eph 4:25-32), lack of love and compassion (Phil 4:2-3). 4. Disunity and division, 1 Corinthians (preferences for different leaders Paul, Apollos, Cephas). 5. Persecution and opposition. 6. Teaching on Christian living, love, humility, forgiveness, generosity, prayer. 7. Concerns about the end times, Thessalonians.

Dating among believers (27-July-23): wouldn't discourage it because a lot of youngsters will take that route (not to be encouraged), Non-Negotiable.

Stand on alcohol consumption.

Things to finalize: Head coverings, done. Role of Women, silence (of leading) in the presence of men, done. Dress code, business casual for leadership given the heart of demonstrating godliness, done. Dates, Jan 14th. Potential breakfast with prospective member on Monday the 6th. Vision Goals. Core values: a. Bible, Expository Preaching and Teaching. b. Christ Centred, Lord's Supper. c. Fellowship, Holistic small groups, Loving relationships. d. Evangelism. e. Discipleship. f. Worship. g. Developing and empowering leadership. h. Gift oriented ministry. i. Prayer. j. Mission focused. k. Good works (Social work). RSVP for Inauguration program and have a letter signed by the elders. Place and name and registration, proposed regional Bible Fellowship name. Trustee options: candidates listed (names redacted).

Open topics: Holy Spirit; Worship.

App/books for music: facilitate thematic selection; create PDF; eventually print hard copies; start with 50 and see if we can ask for old books; permission to print another fellowship's PDF; produce our own hard copy after 6 months; print 10 copies of another fellowship's song book; obtain Songs of Zion books.

Sunday school. Airplane Mode and silence phones.

To Do: Prep the letter. Send text to elders of other assemblies (one elder of another assembly was already informed); send to other churches; wait for 3-4 days and send to other. Plan to inaugural program. Plan sermon/teaching for the first two months. See if we can get website before we start. Have a meeting of all those who want to join, to tell how we will function as church, head covering; statement of faith on marriage; dress code (business casual for leadership given the heart of demonstrating godliness).

For a prospective baptismal candidate: topics to cover before baptism, statement of faith; church practices; disciplines; marriage; baptism. A side conversation about a relational situation is also planned. Talk to the candidate after we are back.

Preaching and teaching for the next three months: 2nd meeting on the statement of faith, maybe the last week singing and testimony time. 1st sermon, continuation on John. Bible Study and sermon, John, the same person taking it; will continue from where we are.
parsing_notes:
ANONYMIZATION: All personal initials and all proper names of contributors that appeared in the source have been stripped from every field. Additional personal names mentioned in passing in the source (assigned-family names, prospective baptismal candidate, trustee candidate names, an elder of another assembly named in the to-do list) have also been redacted from chunk content and the raw_extracted_text, replaced by neutral descriptors like 'a prospective baptismal candidate' or 'one of two named venues'. The specific proposed church name has been generalized to 'a regional Bible Fellowship name'. The external aligned assembly is referred to only as 'an external assembly's statement' / 'an aligned external assembly's statement'. A second referenced fellowship in the song-book section has been generalized to 'another fellowship'. The neighborhood / city abbreviations originally tied to specific contributors have also been generalized to 'the area' or 'the local city'.

MULTIPLE PERSPECTIVES: This document explicitly carries multiple distinct views in three places, (1) the head-covering / women-speaking section with three contributor write-ups (encoded as topics_to_discuss_31, _32, _33 with neutral 'first contributor / second contributor / third contributor' labels and per-chunk perspective markers where internal alternatives were proposed); (2) the cessationism / continuationism description (topics_to_discuss_39 with perspective_a / perspective_b); (3) the three election positions (topics_to_discuss_30 with perspective_a / perspective_b / perspective_c).

FORMAT NOTES: The source contained a markdown table for the sample Sunday format and an embedded image of handwritten notes on church discipline (image1.jpg), the image is referenced in embedded_assets but not transcribed. External hyperlinks (bible.org, 9marks.org, thegospelcoalition.org, gotquestions.org, plus a Google Docs link) are described in chunks but URLs themselves are summarized rather than verbatim where they could leak the contributor's identity (the Google Docs link parameters could fingerprint the author so the URL was paraphrased).

DATES: Several dated discussion entries are preserved (25th Aug 22, 8th Sept 22, 22 Jan 22, 14th Nov 22, 16/2/23, 27-July-23, 28/7/23, 3/8/23, 1st Aug 23, Jan 14th, Sept 16-17 2023). The session-level estimated_date uses the earliest dated entry (25th Aug 22) since this is a meta-document spanning many meetings.

OPEN-AGENDA NATURE: As anticipated, much of section one ('Things to discuss' items 1-25) consists of terse topic placeholders rather than developed teachings. These are preserved as 'other'-typed chunks so no listed topic is lost from the corpus, with cross_references pointing to the later non-negotiable chunk where each was eventually resolved.

NO LOW-CONFIDENCE EXTRACTION: text_extraction is high, the converted markdown was clean and complete; nothing was illegible.
